name: Blind User
description: The interface speaks before it is seen. Navigation depends on correct semantics, alt text, and consistent structure. Redundant descriptions can be helpful. Surprises—like unlabeled buttons or nested actions—erode confidence. Every element asks one question: 'Will this make sense when heard?'

checks.message: Alt text is the image for someone who cannot see it. Make sure every alt value describes the content or purpose of the image, not just its appearance.
checks.technical: Every must have an alt attribute. Decorative images should use alt="". Informative images need a description of meaning, not appearance.

checks.message: Tables are navigated cell by cell. Without clear headers, every cell arrives without context. A blind user may not know what column they're in until they've gone too far.
checks.technical: Use <th scope='col'> and <th scope='row'> to associate headers. Add a <caption> to describe the table's purpose.

checks.message: Form fields need labels that are programmatically associated, not just visually nearby. A blind user hears the label before typing—if it's missing, they're guessing.
checks.technical: Every form input must have an associated <label for='...'> or aria-label. Do not rely on placeholder text as a label substitute.

checks.message: If a click event is attached to a non-interactive element like a div or span, it won't be reachable by keyboard or announced correctly by a screen reader.
checks.technical: Attach click handlers to native interactive elements (<button>, <a>). If a custom element must be used, add role='button' and tabindex='0' and handle keyboard events.

checks.message: Content hidden with display:none is invisible to screen readers too. If you're hiding decorative content, that's fine. If it carries meaning, it needs a different approach.
checks.technical: Use display:none only for content that should be completely hidden from all users. For visually hidden but screen-reader-accessible content, use a visually-hidden utility class instead.

checks.message: aria-hidden removes content from the accessibility tree entirely. Use it carefully—applied to the wrong element, it can hide navigation, labels or critical context from a blind user.
checks.technical: Never apply aria-hidden='true' to focusable elements. Audit all aria-hidden usage to confirm it is applied only to decorative or redundant content.

checks.message: SVG icons and illustrations are often invisible to screen readers unless described. A decorative icon is fine unlabelled—but a meaningful one needs a title or aria-label.
checks.technical: Add a <title> inside meaningful SVGs and reference it with aria-labelledby. For decorative SVGs, use aria-hidden='true'.

checks.message: Iframes are announced by their title, not their contents. Without a descriptive title attribute, a blind user lands inside an unlabelled frame with no way to orient themselves.
checks.technical: Every <iframe> must have a title attribute that describes its purpose. For embeds that are purely decorative, use aria-hidden='true' on the iframe.
